items.subtopic: Diseases of the Spinal Cord | SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 1)
items.title: Localization of Spinal Cord Lesions
items.content: Identifying the level of a spinal cord lesion is crucial for diagnosis and management. Sensory loss below a specific level indicates damage to the spinothalamic tract on the opposite side, one to two segments higher in unilateral lesions. This sensory level can be determined by assessing responses to pinprick or cold stimuli applied from proximal legs upward.
keyPoints: Sensory loss below a certain level corresponds to pathology at that spinal cord segment.
Unilateral lesion: Sensory fibers cross anterior to the central canal, causing damage one to two segments higher.
Bilateral lesion: Damage occurs at the same level as sensory loss.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · Diseases of the Spinal Cord | SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Spinal Cord | SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 1)
items.title: Characteristics of Transverse Spinal Cord Lesions
items.content: Transverse spinal cord lesions result in paraplegia or quadriplegia, heightened deep tendon reflexes, Babinski signs, and spasticity due to upper motor neuron syndrome. Autonomic disturbances include absent sweating below the lesion level and bladder, bowel, and sexual dysfunction.
keyPoints: Transverse damage causes paralysis (paraplegia/quadriplegia) with heightened deep tendon reflexes and Babinski signs.
Autonomic disturbances: Absent sweating, bladder/bowel/sexual dysfunction below lesion level.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · Diseases of the Spinal Cord | SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Diseases of the Spinal Cord | SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 1)
items.title: Uppermost Level Localization in Spinal Cord Lesions
items.content: The uppermost level of a spinal cord lesion can be localized by segmental signs such as altered sensation, fasciculations, muscle atrophy, or changes in deep tendon reflexes. These signs are most useful when combined with long-tract damage indicators.
keyPoints: Segmental signs: Altered sensation, fasciculations, muscle atrophy, changes in reflexes.
Useful for localization when combined with long-tract damage signs.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · Diseases of the Spinal Cord | SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 1)

items.subtopic: SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 2)
items.title: Identifying Thoracic Cord Lesions
items.content: Thoracic cord lesions can be localized using sensory levels on the trunk, such as the nipples (T4) and umbilicus (T10). Beevor's sign is indicative of a lesion at T9–T10, where the lower abdominal muscles are paralyzed but not the upper ones, causing upward movement of the umbilicus during contraction.
keyPoints: Sensory level markers: nipples (T4), umbilicus (T10)
Beevor's sign: lesion at T9–T10
Symptoms include leg weakness and bladder/bowel dysfunction
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 2)

items.subtopic: SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 2)
items.title: Recognizing Conus Medullaris Syndrome
items.content: Conus medullaris syndrome is characterized by bilateral saddle anesthesia, bladder and bowel dysfunction, and absent bulbocavernosus and anal reflexes. Muscle strength remains largely preserved.
keyPoints: Bilateral saddle anesthesia (S3–S5)
Bladder/bowel dysfunction: urinary retention, incontinence
Absent bulbocavernosus (S2–S4) and anal (S4–S5) reflexes
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 2)

items.subtopic: SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 2)
items.title: Understanding Central Cord Syndrome
items.content: Central cord syndrome results from damage to the gray matter and crossing spinothalamic tracts, leading to arm weakness disproportionate to leg weakness and a dissociated sensory loss in a cape distribution.
keyPoints: Arm weakness > leg weakness
Dissociated sensory loss: pain/temperature over shoulders/lower neck/upper trunk
Causes: spinal trauma, syringomyelia, intrinsic cord tumors
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 2)

items.subtopic: SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 3) | ACUTE AND SUBACUTE SPINAL CORD DISEASES | DISTINGUISHING COMPRESSIVE FROM NONCOMPRESSIVE MYELOPATHY
items.title: Differentiating Intramedullary from Extramedullary Lesions
items.content: Intramedullary lesions, arising within the spinal cord, typically cause poorly localized burning pain and sacral sparing due to the laminated configuration of spinothalamic fibers. In contrast, extramedullary lesions, which compress the spinal cord or its vascular supply from outside, often present with radicular pain, early sacral sensory loss, and spastic weakness in the legs.
keyPoints: Intramedullary lesions: poorly localized burning pain, sacral sparing
Extramedullary lesions: radicular pain, early sacral sensory loss, spastic weakness, bladder disturbances
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 3) | ACUTE AND SUBACUTE SPINAL CORD DISEASES | DISTINGUISHING COMPRESSIVE FROM NONCOMPRESSIVE MYELOPATHY

items.subtopic: SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 3) | ACUTE AND SUBACUTE SPINAL CORD DISEASES | DISTINGUISHING COMPRESSIVE FROM NONCOMPRESSIVE MYELOPATHY
items.title: Identifying Compressive vs. Noncompressive Myelopathy
items.content: The initial step in evaluating acute myelopathy is to exclude treatable compressive causes such as tumors, epidural abscesses or hematomas, herniated disks, and spondylitic vertebral pathology. MRI with gadolinium is the preferred diagnostic tool, often imaging the entire spine from cervical through sacral regions to identify clinically silent lesions.
keyPoints: Exclude treatable compression: tumor, epidural abscess/hematoma, herniated disk, spondylitis
MRI with gadolinium: initial diagnostic procedure, image entire spine if possible
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · SPINAL CORD ANATOMY RELEVANT TO CLINICAL SIGNS (part 3) | ACUTE AND SUBACUTE SPINAL CORD DISEASES | DISTINGUISHING COMPRESSIVE FROM NONCOMPRESSIVE MYELOPATHY

items.subtopic: COMPRESSIVE MYELOPATHIES
items.title: Why MRI is Preferred for Diagnosing Spinal Metastases
items.content: MRI provides superior anatomic resolution and differentiation between malignant lesions and other masses such as epidural abscesses or tuberculomas. It can identify vertebral metastases that are hypointense on T1-weighted images, although gadolinium contrast may alter the appearance.
keyPoints: MRI is preferred for diagnosing spinal metastases due to its ability to distinguish between malignant lesions and other masses.
Vertebral metastases appear hypointense relative to normal bone marrow on T1-weighted MRI.
Gadolinium contrast can deceptively normalize the appearance of tumors by increasing their intensity.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · COMPRESSIVE MYELOPATHIES

items.subtopic: COMPRESSIVE MYELOPATHIES
items.title: When to Image for Spinal Metastases and Considerations
items.content: Imaging should be obtained promptly if spinal cord compression is suspected. If radicular symptoms are present without myelopathy, imaging can be deferred for 24–48 hours. Imaging the entire spine is crucial as up to 40% of patients with symptomatic cord compression have asymptomatic metastases elsewhere.
keyPoints: Prompt imaging is necessary if spinal cord compression is suspected.
Imaging may be deferred for 24-48 hours in cases with radicular symptoms but no myelopathy.
Up to 40% of patients with symptomatic cord compression have asymptomatic metastases elsewhere, necessitating full spine imaging.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · COMPRESSIVE MYELOPATHIES

items.subtopic: Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 1)
items.title: Management of Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression
items.content: Treatment involves glucocorticoids, surgery, and/or radiotherapy. Dexamethasone is used to reduce edema, and treatment decisions depend on tumor radiosensitivity, extent of compression, prior therapy, and spinal stability.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoids (dexamethasone 10 mg IV) are administered before imaging if cord compression is suspected; continued at 4 mg every 6 hours orally until definitive treatment.

Surgery followed by radiotherapy is more effective than radiotherapy alone for single-area extradural tumor compression, excluding patients with recurrent compression, brain metastases, radiosensitive tumors, or severe motor symptoms >48 h.
Stereotactic body radiotherapy is preferred for radioresistant tumors and re-irradiation cases.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 1)

items.subtopic: Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 1)
items.title: Indications for Surgical Intervention in Spinal Cord Compression
items.content: Surgery is indicated when there's worsening cord compression despite radiotherapy, maximum-tolerated dose of radiotherapy has been reached, or due to vertebral fracture/spinal instability.
keyPoints: Biopsy of the epidural mass is unnecessary with known primary cancer; required if no history of underlying cancer.

Surgical treatment indicated for worsening signs despite radiotherapy, maximum-tolerated dose delivered, vertebral compression fracture, spinal instability, or high-grade compression from a radioresistant tumor.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 1)

items.subtopic: Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 1)
items.title: Prognosis and Recovery in Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression
items.content: Ambulatory patients at presentation have a good response to therapy, preventing new weakness. Motor recovery occurs in up to one-third of patients; deficits established for >12 hours rarely improve.
keyPoints: Motor deficits (paraplegia or quadriplegia) after >12 h do not usually improve; prognosis for substantial motor recovery is poor beyond 48 h.
Most patients do not experience recurrences in months following radiotherapy, but recurrence becomes likely with survival beyond 2 years.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 1)

items.subtopic: Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 2) | Spinal Epidural Abscess
items.title: Diagnosis and Initial Management of Spinal Epidural Abscess
items.content: Spinal epidural abscesses are primarily diagnosed using MRI to localize the abscess and exclude other causes. Blood cultures, positive in over half of cases, guide initial empirical antibiotic therapy. Direct aspiration during surgery may be necessary for microbiologic diagnosis.
keyPoints: MRI is essential for localization and exclusion of other myelopathy causes.
Blood cultures are positive in >50% of cases.
Direct abscess aspiration often required for definitive microbiologic diagnosis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 2) | Spinal Epidural Abscess

items.subtopic: Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 2) | Spinal Epidural Abscess
items.title: Treatment Strategy for Spinal Epidural Abscess
items.content: The treatment involves decompressive laminectomy with debridement and long-term antibiotic therapy. Empirical antibiotics include vancomycin, ceftriaxone, and metronidazole, adjusted based on culture results. Surgery is preferred unless contraindicated or neurologic signs are minimal.
keyPoints: Decompressive laminectomy with debridement is the primary treatment.
Empirical antibiotics: vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg q12h, ceftriaxone 2 g q12h, metronidazole 30 mg/kg/day divided q6h.
Antibiotic therapy typically continues for 6-8 weeks.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 2) | Spinal Epidural Abscess

items.subtopic: Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 2) | Spinal Epidural Abscess
items.title: Prognosis and Recovery in Spinal Epidural Abscess
items.content: With prompt diagnosis and treatment, up to two-thirds of patients experience significant recovery. Surgical intervention is crucial for preventing paralysis or reversing evolving deficits.
keyPoints: Up to two-thirds of patients recover significantly with timely treatment.
Surgery prevents or reverses evolving neurological deficits.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 2) | Spinal Epidural Abscess

items.subtopic: NONCOMPRESSIVE MYELOPATHIES
items.title: Distinguishing Vascular/Ischemic from Inflammatory Causes in Acute Myelopathy
items.content: In acute myelopathy, once compressive causes are excluded, distinguishing between vascular/ischemic and inflammatory/infectious etiologies is crucial. Hyperacute presentations with back or neck pain and an anterior pattern of spinal cord injury suggest ischemia. Inflammatory causes are more likely in subacute cases with systemic symptoms, CSF oligoclonal bands, or multiple discrete MRI lesions.
keyPoints: Hyperacute presentations with back/neck pain and anterior spinal cord injury suggest ischemia.
Inflammation is suggested by subacute onset, systemic symptoms, CSF oligoclonal bands, or multiple discrete spinal cord MRI lesions.
Common inflammatory causes include MS, NMO, sarcoidosis, SLE, Behcet's disease, postinfectious transverse myelitis, and viral infections.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · NONCOMPRESSIVE MYELOPATHIES

items.subtopic: NONCOMPRESSIVE MYELOPATHIES
items.title: Spinal Cord Infarction: Vascular Supply and Risk Factors
items.content: The spinal cord's blood supply is crucial in understanding ischemic myelopathy. The anterior spinal artery, originating from vertebral arteries at the craniocervical junction, receives additional radicular vessels at C6, an upper thoracic level, and most consistently at T11-L2 (artery of Adamkiewicz). These vessels are essential for supplying the anterior two-thirds of each spinal cord segment.
keyPoints: Anterior spinal artery originates from vertebral arteries at craniocervical junction.
Radicular vessels arise at C6, upper thoracic level, and most consistently at T11-L2 (artery of Adamkiewicz).
These vessels supply the anterior two-thirds of each spinal cord segment.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · NONCOMPRESSIVE MYELOPATHIES

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 1)
items.title: When to Perform MRI for Spinal Cord Compression
items.content: MRI with and without contrast is essential to exclude compressive causes when evaluating myelopathy.
keyPoints: Use MRI to assess spinal cord compression.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 1)
items.title: CSF Studies for Differential Diagnosis
items.content: Comprehensive CSF studies are crucial for identifying infectious, inflammatory, and other etiologies of myelopathy.
keyPoints: Include cell count, protein, glucose, IgG index/synthesis rate, oligoclonal bands, VDRL; Gram's stain, acid-fast bacilli, India ink stains; PCR for various viruses; antibody tests for HTLV-1, Borrelia burgdorferi, Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Chlamydia pneumoniae; viral, bacterial, mycobacterial, and fungal cultures.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 1)
items.title: Identifying Vascular Causes with Imaging
items.content: MRI and CT myelogram are used to evaluate vascular causes of myelopathy, including spinal cord ischemia.
keyPoints: Use MRI or CT myelogram for suspected vascular etiologies.
Spinal angiogram may be necessary for detailed vascular assessment.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 2)
items.title: Management of Spinal Cord Infarction
items.content: In cases of spinal cord infarction due to presumed thromboembolism, acute anticoagulation is generally not indicated except for unusual transient ischemic attacks or incomplete infarctions with a stuttering or progressive course. Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome should be treated with anticoagulation.
keyPoints: Acute anticoagulation is usually not recommended for cord infarction due to thromboembolism, except in specific cases like transient ischemic attacks or incomplete infarctions with progressive symptoms.
Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome requires anticoagulation treatment.
Increasing systemic blood pressure (>90 mmHg) and lumbar drainage of spinal fluid may help but lack systematic study.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 2)
items.title: Prognosis and Treatment of MS-associated Acute Myelitis
items.content: MS can present with acute myelitis, particularly in individuals of Asian or African ancestry. MRI findings typically show mild swelling and abnormal T2-weighted signal areas. Risk factors for conversion to MS include age <40 years, inflammatory CSF, and more than three periventricular lesions on brain MRI.
keyPoints: MS-associated myelitis often presents with mild cord swelling and multifocal abnormal signals on T2-weighted MRI sequences.
68% of patients with partial myelitis develop MS after a mean follow-up of 4 years.
Risk factors for conversion to MS include age <40, inflammatory CSF, and >3 periventricular lesions.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 2)
items.title: Characteristics and Treatment of Neuromyelitis Optica
items.content: NMO is characterized by longitudinally extensive myelopathy, often associated with optic neuritis. Diagnostic serum autoantibodies against AQP-4 are present in 90% of patients. Acute relapses are treated with glucocorticoids and plasma exchange for severe cases.
keyPoints: NMO typically involves longitudinally extensive lesions spanning three or more vertebral segments.
AQP-4 autoantibodies are found in 90% of NMO patients; MOG antibodies may be present in AQP-4-negative cases.
Treatment includes glucocorticoids and plasma exchange for severe relapses, with prophylactic options like eculizumab, inebilizumab, and satralizumab.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 3)
items.title: Diagnosing Sarcoidosis Myelopathy
items.content: Sarcoid myelopathy can present with sensory predominance and MRI findings mimicking tumors or NMO. The characteristic 'trident sign' on MRI, mild lymphocyte-predominant pleocytosis in CSF, and hypoglycorrhachia help distinguish it from other causes.
keyPoints: MRI may show edematous swelling of the spinal cord with subpial gadolinium enhancement producing a 'trident sign'.
CSF profile: mild lymphocyte-predominant pleocytosis and elevated protein level; reduced glucose in minority cases.
Hypoglycorrhachia can help distinguish neurosarcoid from other noninfectious myelitis causes.
Diagnosis is challenging when systemic sarcoid manifestations are minor or absent (nearly 50% of cases).
Initial treatment involves high-dose glucocorticoids, tapered slowly with monitoring.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 3)
items.title: Managing Systemic Immune-Mediated Myelitis
items.content: In SLE-associated myelitis, particularly with AQP-4 antibodies, treatment parallels NMO management. Severe cases unresponsive to glucocorticoids may require plasma exchange.
keyPoints: SLE myelitis often associated with AQP-4 antibodies; treated as for NMO.
Antiphospholipid antibodies' role in SLE myelitis is uncertain due to similar frequency in patients with and without myelitis.
Severe episodes unresponsive to glucocorticoids may require plasma exchange.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 3)
items.title: Understanding Postinfectious Myelitis
items.content: Postinfectious myelitis often follows infections like EBV, CMV, or SARS-CoV-2 and is presumed autoimmune. Treatment typically involves glucocorticoids or plasma exchange in severe cases.
keyPoints: Myelitis can follow infections such as EBV, CMV, mycoplasma, influenza, measles, varicella, mumps, yellow fever, and SARS-CoV-2.
Characterized by onset during recovery from infection with no isolated infectious agent in CNS or CSF.
Treatment usually involves glucocorticoids; plasma exchange for fulminant cases.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 4)
items.title: Distinguishing Acute Infectious Myelitis and Its Treatment
items.content: Acute infectious myelitis can be caused by various viruses, including herpes zoster, HSV types 1 and 2, EBV, CMV, rabies virus, Zika virus, and others. Distinguishing between direct viral infection and postinfectious processes is often challenging. Treatment for specific viral causes should begin promptly with antiviral therapy pending laboratory confirmation.
keyPoints: Herpes zoster can cause myelitis through direct spread from dorsal root ganglia.
HSV-2 (and less commonly HSV-1) leads to Elsberg's syndrome, characterized by recurrent sacral and cauda equina neuritis.
Poliomyelitis affects the anterior gray matter of the spinal cord.
Enterovirus A-71 and D-68 have been associated with acute flaccid paralysis since 2012.
Treatment for herpes zoster, HSV, and EBV myelitis includes intravenous acyclovir (10 mg/kg q8h) or oral valacyclovir (2 g tid) for 10–14 days.
CMV myelitis is treated with ganciclovir (5 mg/kg IV bid) plus foscarnet (60 mg/kg IV tid) or cidofovir (5 mg/kg per week).
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 4)
items.title: Understanding Non-Viral Causes and High-Voltage Electrical Injury in Myelopathy
items.content: Bacterial, mycobacterial, parasitic, and other non-viral causes can lead to myelitis. Schistosomiasis and toxoplasmosis are notable parasitic causes, especially in endemic areas or immunocompromised patients like those with AIDS. High-voltage electrical injuries from lightning strikes or accidents may result in transient weakness followed by severe and permanent myelopathy due to vascular pathology.
keyPoints: Bacterial and mycobacterial myelitis are less common than viral causes.
Schistosomiasis leads to inflammatory granulomatous myelitis, primarily from Schistosoma haematobium or mansoni.
Toxoplasmosis can cause focal myelopathy in AIDS patients.
High-voltage electrical injuries may result in severe and permanent myelopathy due to anterior spinal artery involvement.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 4)

items.subtopic: SPONDYLOTIC MYELOPATHY
items.title: Diagnosis and Imaging for Spondylotic Myelopathy
items.content: Spondylotic myelopathy is diagnosed primarily using MRI, which can reveal extrinsic cord compression and deformation. CT images may also suggest the diagnosis, while plain x-rays are less informative.
keyPoints: MRI is crucial for diagnosing spondylotic myelopathy by showing extrinsic cord compression.
CT scans might suggest the condition but are not definitive.
Plain x-rays have limited utility in diagnosing this condition.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · SPONDYLOTIC MYELOPATHY

items.subtopic: SPONDYLOTIC MYELOPATHY
items.title: Progression and Treatment of Spondylotic Myelopathy
items.content: Medically treated spondylotic myelopathy has a high likelihood of progression, estimated at 8% over one year. Surgical decompression is the definitive treatment.
keyPoints: The progression rate for medically managed myelopathy is approximately 8% per year.
Surgical options include posterior laminectomy or anterior resection of protruded disk and bony material.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · SPONDYLOTIC MYELOPATHY

items.subtopic: VASCULAR MALFORMATIONS OF THE CORD AND DURA
items.title: Identifying and Diagnosing Vascular Malformations in Progressive Myelopathy
items.content: Vascular malformations, such as dural arteriovenous fistulas, are treatable causes of progressive myelopathy. They often present with a slowly worsening or intermittent myelopathy that may mimic multiple sclerosis (MS). Key diagnostic features include incomplete sensory and motor disturbances, bladder issues, and pain over the dorsal spine. Symptoms can change with posture, exertion, Valsalva maneuver, or menses.
keyPoints: Vascular malformations account for ~4% of all mass lesions of the cord and dura.
Most common are fistulas located within the dura or posteriorly along the surface of the cord.
Typical presentation includes progressive myelopathy in middle-aged men, sometimes mimicking MS.
Symptoms may include intermittent claudication, posture-dependent changes, exertion-related changes, Valsalva maneuver effects, menses-related changes, and fever.
Definitive diagnosis requires selective spinal angiography.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · VASCULAR MALFORMATIONS OF THE CORD AND DURA

items.subtopic: VASCULAR MALFORMATIONS OF THE CORD AND DURA
items.title: Diagnostic Imaging and Treatment Approaches for Vascular Malformations
items.content: For suspected vascular malformations, MR angiography and CT angiography can detect draining vessels. Definitive diagnosis is achieved through selective spinal angiography, which identifies feeding vessels and the extent of the malformation. Treatment involves microsurgical resection or endovascular embolization, tailored to the lesion's anatomy and location.
keyPoints: MR angiography and CT angiography can detect draining vessels of many AVMs.
Definitive diagnosis requires selective spinal angiography.
Treatment generally consists of microsurgical resection or endovascular embolization, or a combination.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · VASCULAR MALFORMATIONS OF THE CORD AND DURA

items.subtopic: RETROVIRUS-ASSOCIATED MYELOPATHIES
items.title: Diagnosis and Progression of HTLV-1 Myelopathy
items.content: HTLV-1 myelopathy, previously known as tropical spastic paraparesis, is a slowly progressive condition characterized by spasticity, sensory disturbances, and bladder issues. Diagnosis involves detecting HTLV-1-specific antibodies in serum using ELISA, confirmed with radioimmunoprecipitation or Western blot analysis. The CSF/serum antibody index can support diagnosis by indicating intrathecal synthesis of antibodies. Disease progression is variable; median times to cane-, walker-, and wheelchair-dependence are 6, 13, and 21 years respectively.
keyPoints: HTLV-1 myelopathy presents with spastic syndrome, sensory disturbances, and bladder issues.
Diagnosis confirmed by HTLV-1-specific antibody detection in serum via ELISA, radioimmunoprecipitation, or Western blot.
CSF/serum antibody index supports diagnosis by showing intrathecal synthesis of antibodies.
Median progression times: 6 years to cane-dependence, 13 years to walker-dependence, and 21 years to wheelchair-dependence.
Progression is faster in older patients and those with higher viral loads.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · RETROVIRUS-ASSOCIATED MYELOPATHIES

items.subtopic: RETROVIRUS-ASSOCIATED MYELOPATHIES
items.title: Management Strategies for HTLV-1 Myelopathy
items.content: There is no proven effective treatment for HTLV-1 myelopathy. Systemic glucocorticoids, involving pulsed high-dose induction followed by low-dose chronic maintenance, may be tried based on limited evidence. Mogamulizumab, a monoclonal antibody targeting CCR4, has shown preliminary success in slowing progression and reducing neurological disability.
keyPoints: No proven effective treatment for HTLV-1 myelopathy.
Systemic glucocorticoids (pulsed high-dose induction followed by low-dose maintenance) may be tried based on limited evidence.
Mogamulizumab, targeting CCR4, has shown preliminary success in slowing progression and reducing disability.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · RETROVIRUS-ASSOCIATED MYELOPATHIES

items.subtopic: SYRINGOMYELIA
items.title: Clinical Presentation and Progression of Syringomyelia
items.content: Syringomyelia typically presents insidiously in adolescence or early adulthood with symptoms that progress irregularly. It often begins asymmetrically, causing unilateral sensory loss in the hands leading to unnoticed injuries and burns due to dissociated sensory loss (loss of pain and temperature sensation while sparing touch and vibration). As the condition progresses, muscle wasting and areflexic weakness occur in the upper limbs, followed by spasticity and weakness in the legs, bladder and bowel dysfunction, and Horner's syndrome as the cavity enlarges.
keyPoints: Symptoms begin insidiously in adolescence or early adulthood.
Progression is irregular with possible spontaneous arrest for years.
More than half of cases are associated with Chiari type 1 malformations.
Presentation includes dissociated sensory loss and areflexic weakness in upper limbs, often starting asymmetrically.
Expansion leads to spasticity, leg weakness, bladder/bowel dysfunction, Horner's syndrome.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · SYRINGOMYELIA

items.subtopic: SYRINGOMYELIA
items.title: Imaging and Associated Conditions in Syringomyelia
items.content: MRI is the diagnostic modality of choice for identifying syrinx cavities. It should include images of both the brain and entire spinal cord to assess the full extent of the syrinx, evaluate posterior fossa structures for Chiari malformations, and check for hydrocephalus. Syringomyelia can be associated with acquired cavitations following trauma, myelitis, necrotic spinal tumors, or chronic arachnoiditis.
keyPoints: MRI accurately identifies syrinx cavities and associated spinal cord enlargement.
Imaging should cover the brain and entire spinal cord to assess Chiari malformations and hydrocephalus.
Acquired cavitations can follow trauma, myelitis, necrotic tumors, or chronic arachnoiditis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · SYRINGOMYELIA

items.subtopic: Syringomyelia | CHRONIC MYELOPATHY OF MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS | SUBACUTE COMBINED DEGENERATION (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $ DEFICIENCY)
items.title: When to Consider Surgical Intervention in Syringomyelia
items.content: Surgical decompression is the primary treatment for syringomyelia, especially when symptoms are present. The procedure may involve suboccipital cranectomy and upper cervical laminectomy with dural graft placement to reestablish fourth ventricular outflow. Direct decompression or drainage of large syrinx cavities can be considered but lacks proven added benefits and carries higher complication risks.
keyPoints: Surgical decompression is the treatment of choice for symptomatic syringomyelia.
Chiari tonsillar herniation may require suboccipital cranectomy, upper cervical laminectomy, and dural graft placement.
Direct decompression or drainage has not demonstrated added benefits and carries common complications.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · Syringomyelia | CHRONIC MYELOPATHY OF MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS | SUBACUTE COMBINED DEGENERATION (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $ DEFICIENCY)

items.subtopic: Syringomyelia | CHRONIC MYELOPATHY OF MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS | SUBACUTE COMBINED DEGENERATION (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $ DEFICIENCY)
items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Chronic Myelopathy in Multiple Sclerosis
items.content: Chronic myelopathy is a frequent cause of disability in progressive forms of MS, characterized by bilateral but asymmetric motor, sensory, and bladder/bowel disturbances. Diagnosis is supported by MRI, CSF analysis, and evoked-response testing. Ocrelizumab is effective for primary progressive MS, while disease-modifying therapy is recommended for secondary progressive MS with active disease.
keyPoints: Chronic myelopathy in MS presents with motor, sensory, and bladder/bowel disturbances.
Diagnosis involves MRI, CSF analysis, and evoked-response testing.
Ocrelizumab is effective for primary progressive MS; disease-modifying therapy is indicated for active secondary progressive MS.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · Syringomyelia | CHRONIC MYELOPATHY OF MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS | SUBACUTE COMBINED DEGENERATION (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $ DEFICIENCY)

items.subtopic: Syringomyelia | CHRONIC MYELOPATHY OF MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS | SUBACUTE COMBINED DEGENERATION (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $ DEFICIENCY)
items.title: Clinical Presentation and Treatment of Subacute Combined Degeneration
items.content: Subacute combined degeneration due to vitamin B12 deficiency presents with subacute paresthesias, loss of vibration/position sensation, spastic weakness, and ataxia. Diagnostic clues include Babinski signs with peripheral neuropathy. Diagnosis is confirmed by macrocytic red blood cells, low serum B12 levels, and elevated homocysteine/methylmalonic acid. Treatment involves intramuscular vitamin B12 replacement followed by oral maintenance.
keyPoints: Subacute combined degeneration presents with paresthesias, loss of sensation, spastic weakness, and ataxia.
Diagnostic confirmation includes macrocytic red blood cells, low serum B12, elevated homocysteine/methylmalonic acid.
Treatment starts with 1000 µg intramuscular vitamin B12 daily for 5–7 days, transitioning to weekly doses, then monthly maintenance.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · Syringomyelia | CHRONIC MYELOPATHY OF MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS | SUBACUTE COMBINED DEGENERATION (VITAMIN B $ _{12} $ DEFICIENCY)

items.subtopic: HYPOCUPRIC MYELOPATHY
items.title: Recognizing Hypocupric Myelopathy: Causes and Treatment
items.content: Hypocupric myelopathy presents similarly to subacute combined degeneration but with normal B12 levels. It is associated with low serum copper and ceruloplasmin, often following gastrointestinal procedures or zinc excess from supplements. The condition can be idiopathic and may coexist with anemia.
keyPoints: Low serum copper and ceruloplasmin are characteristic.
Potential causes include impaired copper absorption post-bariatric surgery or excessive zinc intake.
Oral supplementation of 2 mg/d of copper can lead to improvement or stabilization.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · HYPOCUPRIC MYELOPATHY | TABES DORSALIS | HEREDITARY SPASTIC PARAPLEGIA

items.subtopic: TABES DORSALIS
items.title: Diagnosing Tabes Dorsalis: Key Symptoms and Treatment
items.content: Tabes dorsalis, a form of neurosyphilis, is characterized by lancinating pains, ataxia due to loss of position sense, paresthesias, bladder disturbances, and visceral crises. Bilateral Argyll Robertson pupils are almost always present. Diagnosis should consider diabetic polyradiculopathy as a differential.
keyPoints: Symptoms include fleeting, repetitive lancinating pains and ataxia in half of patients.
15–30% experience bladder disturbances and acute abdominal pain with vomiting.
Argyll Robertson pupils are a cardinal sign.
Treatment involves penicillin G administered intravenously or intramuscularly with oral probenecid.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · HYPOCUPRIC MYELOPATHY | TABES DORSALIS | HEREDITARY SPASTIC PARAPLEGIA

items.subtopic: PRIMARY LATERAL SCLEROSIS | ADRENOMYELONEUROPATHY | CANCER-RELATED SYNDROMES
items.title: Primary Lateral Sclerosis: Differentiating from ALS and Hereditary Spastic Paraplegia
items.content: Primary lateral sclerosis (PLS) is a mid- to late-life-onset degenerative disorder characterized by progressive spasticity with weakness, dysarthria, and dysphonia. Unlike amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), PLS lacks lower motor neuron involvement and progresses more slowly. It can be mistaken for hereditary spastic paraplegia, especially in late-onset cases without a family history.
keyPoints: Progressive spasticity with weakness, dysarthria, and dysphonia
Bladder symptoms occur in approximately half of patients
Sensory function is spared
No lower motor neuron disturbance
Slower progression compared to ALS
May represent late-onset hereditary spastic paraplegia
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · PRIMARY LATERAL SCLEROSIS | ADRENOMYELONEUROPATHY | CANCER-RELATED SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: PRIMARY LATERAL SCLEROSIS | ADRENOMYELONEUROPATHY | CANCER-RELATED SYNDROMES
items.title: Adrenomyeloneuropathy: Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Adrenomyeloneuropathy (AMN) is an X-linked peroxisomal disorder, a variant of adrenoleukodystrophy. It presents with adrenal insufficiency followed by progressive spastic or ataxic paraparesis in males, often starting in early to middle adulthood. Diagnosis involves elevated very-long-chain fatty acids in plasma and cultured fibroblasts. Treatment includes corticosteroid replacement for hypoadrenalism.
keyPoints: X-linked peroxisomal disorder
Variant of adrenoleukodystrophy (ALD)
History of adrenal insufficiency followed by progressive spastic/ataxic paraparesis
Elevated very-long-chain fatty acids in plasma and cultured fibroblasts for diagnosis
Corticosteroid replacement indicated if hypoadrenalism is present
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · PRIMARY LATERAL SCLEROSIS | ADRENOMYELONEUROPATHY | CANCER-RELATED SYNDROMES

items.subtopic: OTHER CHRONIC MYELOPATHIES
items.title: Tethered Cord Syndrome: Recognizing Congenital Clues and Confirming with MRI
items.content: Tethered cord syndrome is a developmental disorder often presenting in adulthood as low back pain with progressive lower spinal cord or nerve root symptoms. Key congenital clues include leg or foot deformities, dimples, patches of hair, or sinus tracts over the lower back. Diagnosis is confirmed via MRI showing a low-lying conus medullaris and thickened filum terminale, potentially revealing coexisting abnormalities like diastematomyelia, lipomas, or cysts.
keyPoints: Low back pain with progressive lower spinal cord/nerve root syndrome
Congenital clues: leg/foot deformity, dimple, hair patch, sinus tract over lower back
MRI findings: low-lying conus medullaris, thickened filum terminale
Possible coexisting abnormalities: diastematomyelia, lipomas, cysts
Treatment involves surgical release
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · OTHER CHRONIC MYELOPATHIES

items.subtopic: OTHER CHRONIC MYELOPATHIES
items.title: Toxic Causes of Spastic Myelopathy: Identifying Rare Etiologies
items.content: Rare toxic causes of spastic myelopathy include lathyrism and Konzo. Lathyrism results from ingestion of chickpeas containing the excitotoxin $\beta$-N-oxalylamino-L-alanine (BOAA), primarily seen in developing regions or during famines. Konzo is caused by consuming cyanogen-containing cassava, prevalent in sub-Saharan Africa.
keyPoints: Lathyrism: ingestion of chickpeas with excitotoxin $\beta$-N-oxalylamino-L-alanine (BOAA)
Geographic prevalence: developing world, famines
Konzo: ingestion of cyanogen-containing cassava
Geographic prevalence: sub-Saharan Africa
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · OTHER CHRONIC MYELOPATHIES

items.subtopic: REHABILITATION OF SPINAL CORD DISORDERS
items.title: Rehabilitation Goals and Considerations Post-Spinal Cord Injury
items.content: The rehabilitation of spinal cord disorders focuses on developing a plan with realistic expectations, addressing neurologic, medical, and psychological complications. The level of the lesion and completeness of function disturbance are primary determinants of disability.
keyPoints: Recovery prospects fade after ~6 months post-acute destructive spinal cord lesion.
Disability is determined by lesion level and whether the function disturbance is complete or incomplete.
Realistic rehabilitation plans should address neurologic, medical, and psychological complications.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · REHABILITATION OF SPINAL CORD DISORDERS

items.subtopic: REHABILITATION OF SPINAL CORD DISORDERS
items.title: Management of Bladder Dysfunction in Spinal Cord Injury
items.content: Bladder dysfunction post-spinal cord injury is managed through pharmacological and surgical interventions. Anticholinergic drugs treat detrusor spasticity, while $\alpha$-adrenergic blockers manage urinary dyssynergia.
keyPoints: Detrusor spasticity treated with anticholinergics (oxybutynin 2.5–5 mg qid) or tricyclic antidepressants.
$\alpha$-adrenergic blocking agent terazosin hydrochloride (1–2 mg tid or qid) for urinary dyssynergia.
Surgical options include enterocystoplasty and creation of a urinary conduit.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · REHABILITATION OF SPINAL CORD DISORDERS

items.subtopic: REHABILITATION OF SPINAL CORD DISORDERS
items.title: Prevention and Management of Spasticity in Spinal Cord Injury
items.content: Spasticity management involves stretching exercises, pharmacological treatments, and advanced interventions like intrathecal baclofen or botulinum toxin injections for refractory cases.
keyPoints: Stretching exercises maintain joint mobility.
Baclofen (up to 240 mg/d) facilitates $\gamma$-aminobutyric acid-mediated inhibition of motor reflex arcs.
Intrathecal baclofen, botulinum toxin injections, or dorsal rhizotomy for refractory spasticity.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · REHABILITATION OF SPINAL CORD DISORDERS

Sensory loss only in the lower limbs

Presence of lower motor neuron disturbance.

items.assertion: Primary intramedullary tumors of the spinal cord often present with sacral sensation sparing.
items.reason: These lesions typically include ependymomas, hemangioblastomas, or low-grade astrocytomas.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why sacral sensation is spared.

items.subtopic: Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 2) | Spinal Epidural Abscess
items.assertion: Prompt diagnosis and treatment of spinal epidural abscess can lead to significant recovery in up to two-thirds of patients.
items.reason: Surgical decompression combined with long-term antibiotic therapy is effective in treating the condition.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why prompt treatment leads to significant recovery.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 2) | Spinal Epidural Abscess

items.subtopic: Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 2) | Spinal Epidural Abscess
items.assertion: Blood cultures are positive in all cases of spinal epidural abscess.
items.reason: Direct aspiration of the abscess at surgery is often required for a microbiologic diagnosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because blood cultures are positive in more than half, not all, cases. The reason is true.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · Neoplastic Spinal Cord Compression (part 2) | Spinal Epidural Abscess

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 1)
items.assertion: Spinal cord ischemia typically occurs at the level of T3-T4.
items.reason: The presence of the artery of Adamkiewicz below and anterior spinal artery circulation above creates a region of marginal blood flow in the upper thoracic segments.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why ischemia typically occurs at T3-T4.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 1)
items.assertion: MRI always shows spinal cord infarctions immediately after they occur.
items.reason: Diffusion-weighted restriction is a feature seen on MRI suggestive of cord infarction.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because MRI may fail to demonstrate infarctions in the first day, though diffusion-weighted restriction can be indicative.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 2)
items.assertion: MS is the most common inflammatory cause of acute myelitis.
items.reason: Involvement by MS is usually partial and not transverse.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why MS is the most common cause.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 2)
items.assertion: NMO is associated with optic neuritis that often precedes or follows myelitis.
items.reason: Optic neuritis in NMO is typically unilateral and mild.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as optic neuritis in NMO is often bilateral.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 3)
items.assertion: Sarcoidosis myelitis can present with a 'trident sign' on MRI.
items.reason: The 'trident sign' is due to subpial gadolinium enhancement and central canal involvement.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 3)
items.assertion: Antiphospholipid antibodies are more frequent in SLE patients with myelitis.
items.reason: The frequency of antiphospholipid antibodies is similar in SLE patients with and without myelitis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason correctly states that antibody frequency does not differ significantly.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · TABLE 453-3 Considerations in the Evaluation of Myelopathy (part 3)

items.subtopic: RETROVIRUS-ASSOCIATED MYELOPATHIES
items.assertion: HTLV-1-associated myelopathy can result from an immune response directed against HTLV-1 antigens in the nervous system.
items.reason: The pathogenesis of HTLV-1-associated myelopathy could also be due to secondary autoimmunity triggered by the viral infection.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for the assertion as both immune response against antigens or secondary autoimmunity can contribute to pathogenesis.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · RETROVIRUS-ASSOCIATED MYELOPATHIES

items.assertion: Subacute combined degeneration is a treatable myelopathy.
items.reason: Treatment involves replacement therapy with vitamin B12.

items.assertion: Ocrelizumab is used to treat subacute combined degeneration.

items.subtopic: REHABILITATION OF SPINAL CORD DISORDERS
items.assertion: Prophylaxis against decubitus ulcers involves frequent position changes and special mattresses.
items.reason: This helps prevent pressure sores in areas like the sacral prominence and heels.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Diseases of the Spinal Cord · REHABILITATION OF SPINAL CORD DISORDERS

items.subtopic: REHABILITATION OF SPINAL CORD DISORDERS
items.assertion: Paroxysmal autonomic hyperreflexia is triggered by stimuli below the level of a spinal cord lesion.
items.reason: It occurs due to lesions above T6 affecting major splanchnic sympathetic outflow.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
